summary: I was born in Laos into a large family of twelve. Around 1975, the country fell to war, which forced us to flee. Life in Laos was difficult, especially for education and opportunities, so leaving was necessary for survival and a better future. Although there were good things in Laos, the lack of resources made it hard to pursue schooling or success.

After fleeing Laos in 1979, my family and I crossed into Thailand where we lived in refugee camps. I worked as a nurse and public health worker helping both refugees and local Thai people. Thailand was a place of refuge but also hardship, as we had to adapt to camp life and limited resources while hoping for resettlement.
cues: worked as nurse and public health worker

summary: From 1979 until 1992, my family and I lived in the Ban Vinai refugee camp in Thailand. There, I attended school, worked first as a nurse, then as a public health worker, and eventually became a coordinator. The camp was a challenging environment but also a place where I gained education and skills that helped me support my community.

After Ban Vinai camp closed in 1992, my family was transferred to the Phanat Nikhon camp. It was there that I registered and interviewed for resettlement to the United States. Phanat Nikhon was a transitional place where I prepared for a new life abroad and worked to bring my family to America for better opportunities.

In 1994, my family and I were resettled to the United States after years in refugee camps. Coming here meant the chance for my children to receive a proper education and pursue their dreams. The US represents hope and opportunity, a place where we can build a better future beyond the hardships we endured in Laos and Thailand.

After arriving in the United States, my family settled in Minnesota. This state became our new home where we work hard to succeed and provide for our children. Minnesota offers a community and environment where we can grow and realize the dreams that were impossible back in the camps.
